text.title: DENTRO LA CITTÀ PIÙ RAZZISTA AL MONDO 🇿🇦
text.u: Our policy is what in Afrikaans is called "apartheid".
And I am afraid it has very often been misunderstood.
It could just as easily, and perhaps much better, be described as a policy of good neighborliness.
We are the descendants of those earlier generations of politicians and people who implemented apartheid,
who dominated South Africa, who governed it rather well.
This is an Afrikaner town, that is what we want to call it one day.
A place where Afrikaners can come and feel at home.
To feel that we belong to this place.
This is our place.
When you get permanent residence,
you give us the right to call you at any time of the day or night to subject you to a drug test.
Here we let the children play in the street without the fear that someone will take them away or run over them with a car.
I do not have to look over my shoulder to see who will grab my bag when I go shopping.
Here we are safe and we are really happy.
My name is Giuseppe and I have a mission:
to explore the most remote corners of the planet
to meet guardians of ancient secrets,
women and men with extraordinary stories
and lives able to inspire us and make us see the world with different eyes.
And to each of them I will ask the most important question of all:
"What does happiness mean to you?"
A question so powerful that it reveals the deepest essence of humanity.
Welcome to "PROJECT HAPPINESS".
This is the most racist town in the world.
Or rather, that is the nickname given to Orania, a village of 3,000 strictly white Christian inhabitants.
For some it is even the last stronghold of apartheid,
while for others it is an example of cultural self determination.
And we do not want to limit ourselves to judging, we want to see, talk, understand.
Because the real question is: can you really live by creating a border with everything that is different?
And what makes a person believe that this is the road to happiness?
Well, we will find out together because today a great "Project Happiness" adventure is about to begin.
Orania is right here, in the middle of nowhere, in the heart of South Africa.
It was born in 1991, when the apartheid regime was collapsing.
Apartheid, an Afrikaans word that means separation, was a political and social system of racial segregation,
that ruled South Africa for almost 50 years.
Imagine a country where whites and blacks lived separated in everything.
Schools, transport, hospitals, beaches, even benches.
But it was not just about separation.
It was systemic inequality.
Laws that guaranteed rights only to one part of the population.
While the other was excluded from everything.
At the end of the 1980s, however, under international pressure and internal uprisings,
the white government understood that it could no longer hold on.
But not everyone was ready for this change.
Some Afrikaners, that is the minority that designed apartheid,
feared that with the end of white supremacy they would lose not only power but also their identity.
And so, a group of families bought an old abandoned construction site on the banks of the Orange River
and began to build a town from zero.
That is how Orania was born, an exclusively Afrikaner community.
Not only white, but culturally Afrikaans.
Here in fact they speak only Afrikaans.
They study Afrikaner history and they practice the Calvinist religion just like a century ago.
Orania is not just a place.
It is an idea.
An idea that you can defend your culture by creating your own world that is separate, closed and protected.
There is something in this place that attracts you and pushes you away at the same time.
In the street everything seems still.
There is no one.
But in reality there are eyes that are following us.
And a simple friendly greeting is enough for us to be welcomed by one of the first inhabitants of Orania.
Here we are safe and we are really happy.
We want to stay among our Afrikaans people, where we can speak Afrikaans,
where we can live our religion, our religious culture.
And beyond that, be safe and protected.
I do not have to look over my shoulder to see who will grab my bag when I go shopping.
Or be anxious about what could happen to us while we sleep at night.
And that is why we came to Orania.
And among the 2,500 inhabitants who live here permanently,
we also meet those who have just arrived with the goal of protecting their child's future.
Here we let the children play in the street without the fear that someone will take them away or run over them with a car.
Here he will definitely have opportunities to grow as a person and to learn our culture in his mother tongue.
And he will be able to use it outside too, if he decides to.
And he will also be a farmer!
And he will also be a farmer!
And then there are also those who think that the only road is to separate from those who are different.
Do you feel more Oranian or South African?
I see myself as an Oranian.
My loyalty starts in Orania and then, partly, also outside.
We do not necessarily see a future for us, culturally, outside Orania.
For that we are in fact South Africans.
We live in South Africa and we have ties all over the country with places that are dear to us.
But first of all we are Oranians
because it is here that we actively take part in the protection and preservation of our heritage.
There are people who would not want us to exist.
What do you think are the main misunderstandings?
The main misunderstanding is that Orania is an organization or a town that is racist, based on race.
Here in Orania, defending your identity does not only mean preserving it, but literally building it.
It is really surprising to walk through the streets of Orania because it really looks like a construction site.
The town is expanding a lot, there are workers, buildings on every corner of the street.
And as you can see here it is full of bricks, they are very special bricks that the workers use here,
they are insulating bricks because here the thermal difference between night and day is very strong.
Especially also between winter and summer, when in winter it goes down to minus 5 degrees and in summer it goes up to plus 45 degrees.
So these workers have to build houses that are habitable, livable for all the people who will arrive.
They are more or less 3,000 people, but the goal is to welcome about 50,000 people in the next years.
So imagine how this little town will change.
And then there is also a very important detail that makes you understand the peculiarity of this little town.
That is that all the structures must be lower than the trees.
Because here the goal, the urban plan is to create a rural town.
Not a town, a megalopolis like Cape Town or like Johannesburg, from which the Afrikaners are running away.
Here the town has to be welcoming, human sized.
We meet one of the engineers who has been given the most ambitious mission.
To transform Orania from a small community into a great nation.
Can you explain why Orania chooses not to employ black labor here?
The reason is that we do not consider it morally acceptable
to exploit the labor of other cultures for our own economic benefit.
We have seen that it did not work.
It does not work.
It is not morally acceptable.
And we had to change that.
Furthermore we boost the economy because we economically empower our own people.
This is an Afrikaner town.
That is what we want to call it one day.
A place where Afrikaners can come and feel at home.
To feel that this is our place.
This is our place.
In Afrikaans we say: "Bou die stad, bou die toekoms."
So build a town to build a future.
This is my mission in Orania.
Now it is clearer to me, for Orania to exist it needs many new citizens.
Afrikaners scattered all over the continent who will answer the call to join this mission.
But it is not enough just to want to live in Orania.
To enter you have to pass a very strict selection by a committee based on three fundamental criteria.
The person is an Afrikaner who speaks Afrikaans fluently.
They are a person who is able to provide for themselves.
We do not have a big government that supports people economically,
so you have to be able to find a job or start a business or have enough savings, and so on.
We check whether they have a criminal record and a drug record.
Is the person coming here to harm our large population of children that we have in Orania?
Or will they be safe from things like drugs, abuse and violence?
And finally, also the cultural aspect.
Do they profess our beliefs? Do they share our Christian faith? And so on.
Even if this could give the impression that it is a very, very strict set of guidelines.
In reality it is a very basic set of guidelines.
And if you want to enter this community, this space for Afrikaners, these are some of the principles that we keep here.
I was imagining the scenario in which I was an Afrikaner who lives in Orania,
but then, while studying abroad, I fell in love with a Xhosa woman, a black woman.
We get married and we come back to Orania.
Can I live in Orania with my wife?
That person therefore is not an Afrikaner person.
All right, well, remember that Orania is a place for Afrikaners.
Many people in these cases would choose a more multicultural town.
Anyone can apply, but then they should convince the council.
What do you think is the main motivation that pushes people to move to Orania?
Is it fear?
Is it anger?
Or is it the fact of living in a complicated country where they no longer feel safe?
Even if these are all reasons why people move to Orania.
The incredible corruption.
The blatant failure of services in South Africa.
The terrible farm murders.
They are definitely all contributing factors and we classify them as push factors.
But I hope and believe that for many the pull factors are more important.
I would like to see a perfect South Africa where there are no potholes in the roads,
where there is no crime, where there is nothing wrong and people still choose Orania.
And before saying goodbye to Joost, we exchange a few euros with the Ora, Orania’s local currency.
It has absolutely no legal value outside here, nor can it in any way replace the South African Rand,
but inside the community it is accepted everywhere.
In practice it is a voucher system created to keep the money inside the community and strengthen self sufficiency.
There is no doubt that safety is one of the reasons why so many choose Orania.
Here there are absolutely no thefts, assaults or organized crime.
But that does not mean there is no one protecting the town.
Police?
Present.
Firefighters?
Also.
Ambulance?
Sure.
But there is just one tiny detail.
It is always the same person answering.
We are the security department.
We are the police.
We are the firefighters.
We are the water department.
Basically, everything that concerns law and order in town, we do it.
I imagine you do not have much work to do because Orania is a very safe place.
Orania is very safe, but we have to make sure it is safe just outside the borders too.
We do not have jurisdiction outside, but it works like a target.
In the center there is Orania.
If Orania is safe, then it will also be safe outside.
That is why we have measures in place.
If you want to come and live here, besides everything else you have to do, we take fingerprints.
And that way we can see whether you have a criminal record or not.
Unfortunately drugs also get here in town, so we do random drug tests.
And when you stay here, when you get permanent residence, you sign it.
And by signing, you authorize us, as law enforcement
to call you at any time of the day or night to subject you to a drug test.
And to move from a social experiment to a real town, there are various stages.
One of these is to build schools, colleges, universities like the one you see behind me,
which however is unique in its kind, because inside there are no desks, there are no books,
but there are small building sites where young people come here to study to become the next electricians,
the next plumbers, the next bricklayers who will build the great Afrikaner city.
I am here and I am here to build.
We are in the middle of the building phase to make Orania better and stronger.
Right now it is an extraordinary time and place to be a citizen of Orania.
It is the most exciting, the most demanding and difficult part of a project and of the community’s and the country’s progress.
And to be part of it, it is a great honor to be part of it now.
I am proud to be Afrikaner and I love our culture.
Certain things happen in South Africa.
I cannot study in my language, for me it is difficult.
That is what makes Orania beautiful.
Here I can study, I can feel comfortable.
I do not want to go to campus and hear some group chanting to kill my culture,
to kill the people who belong to my culture.
If the roles were reversed and I said, "Listen, let us kill this kind of people."
If I sang that song at college and things like that,
they would arrest me very quickly.
So there is a bit of discrimination going on.
Napo, where are you from?
I am Peruvian.
Near Orania?
More or less.
More or less, right!?
Well, Napoleon and I are building the new "Project Happiness" house,
but there is one thing that cannot be missing, and that is protection.
And you already know with what!
With "NordVPN" which protects everything we do online.
It connects you securely, encrypts your data and lets you browse as if you were in another country.
It also blocks viruses, annoying ads and warns you if your credentials end up on the Dark Web.
And the most important thing is that with just one subscription you can protect up to 10 devices,
so the whole team, which is not here now, will be protected.
How long is this broom?
And then it is perfect if you travel or work remotely or you simply want to browse without risks.
It is like having a digital alarm system always on wherever you are.
And as always by scanning this QR code or clicking the link in the description
you can try "NordVPN" with a special offer.
And if you are not satisfied you will have 30 days to change your mind, satisfied or refunded.
And now let us go back to Orania.
There is no doubt that here every citizen is truly proud
to be part of this community, to live in Orania.
In fact you can see it from all the flags that are displayed outside the houses,
outside the buildings, like this one.
And the flag however is very symbolic because it recalls the colors of the former South African flag
of 1984, before the end of apartheid.
There is blue, white, yellow, but this one has a special symbol,
it has a man rolling up his sleeves to get to work, to get down to it.
And it is actually a very direct message because here in Orania they want to remind everyone
that this community was built with effort, piece by piece, without external help.
And it is in fact a source of pride for them to be self sufficient and do everything by themselves,
even if it means staying closed off from the rest of the world.
And by looking closely at this flag you understand a lot about Orania,
this need to build walls, literally and symbolically, to defend their identity.
But there is just one man who can really help us understand what Orania is.
His name is Carel Boshoff, none other than the grandson of Hendrik Verwoerd, the father of apartheid.
But above all, the son of the man who founded Orania in the 1990s, while the old South Africa was collapsing.
In a sense, Carel is a man suspended between two worlds,
the world of imposed separation and the world of chosen separation.
I think people might be surprised by the books you have here.
Some might be surprised by them.
Yes, yes, yes, yes.
No, it is... it is interesting.
Mr. Mandela, who visited Orania, was in a certain sense... how could we call it?
He almost became a family friend.
Really?
Well, I am obviously emphasizing it, but yes.
And what did Mandela think of Orania?
I think he believed it was a passing phenomenon.
He did not think it would last?
He did not think it would last.
No, he thought: "All right, if they have to adapt, let them adapt."
He was never, um, destructively hostile towards Orania.
So, for that we are glad.
The first question might sound a bit harsh.
People outside Orania believe this is one of the most racist towns in the world.
What do you think of this label?
Seen from the outside, you only see a group of white people living together,
who are creating a new community by living together.
From our point of view, it is not about being white.
It is about language and culture and about those bonds
that hold people together in a community, even in a wider social community.
We are the descendants of those earlier generations of politicians and people who implemented apartheid,
who dominated South Africa, who governed it rather well.
We are their descendants physically, but also, in some ways, intellectually.
We are not categorically rejecting where we come from, but we are reinventing that way of thinking.
South Africa, for reasons that are not hard to understand, even from our point of view, does not look kindly on us.
In the past we were too dominant.
We were perhaps a bit overbearing with our values and our ways of operating.
The effect, unfortunately, is that even our language, or perhaps precisely our language, is not liked
and it is represented as the language of the oppressor.
We are discriminated against, but that is not the biggest problem...
Bigger than that is that we are being killed.
Our people are being killed.
Right now South Africa is a violent country.
Every day someone of ours is killed on the farms or in the cities, everywhere.
And we are a small people.
It cannot go on like this.
We cannot live like this.
Like this we will not survive.
And that is why, besides other perhaps more idealistic reasons,
we concretely need a safe harbor and a national home for our people.
How do you see the future of Orania?
Western civilization is entering a very demanding phase.
So our best future is to be part of the next phase, the postcolonial phase of Western civilization.
And I pray, hope and work so that Orania will have this role in the future.
What I wonder though is, can a culture survive if it never meets the other?
Or does it simply become a castle built on the idea that everything that is different is a threat?
I go looking for answers with Joost, at the highest and most symbolic point in Orania.
Monument Hill.
And while we chat, as if framing us all around,
there are the busts of the founding fathers of apartheid and of this town.
And there, with the sun setting over the community that he himself is selecting one by one,
I ask him a question that has been bouncing around in my head since I arrived in Orania.
Do you ever feel here that you are disconnected from the rest of the world?
It does not mean we do not see what is happening in the world, both the good and the bad.
It does not mean we do not know people from other places and other tribes and other nations who do things differently from us.
We are aware, we are connected in that sense.
But we are also connected to ourselves, to our people, to our, to our religion.
And we work precisely for this, to have even more connection in this sense.
That is what we dream of.
What do you think your mission here in Orania is?
To be part of Orania’s bigger mission.
And that is to create a home.
We all know it... instinctively we know we need a home.
A home is protection from the elements, it is a safe place, it is a place where you can be yourself.
And that is what Orania has to be, a home for the people.
What does happiness mean here in Orania?
I think that for the people of Orania, happiness means...
having the possibility to have an impact on the future.
To be builders of the future.
Not being extremely worried about the future because you are busy building it.
If the future worries you, create it.
You have to create the future.
And that is why Orania’s symbol is a young man rolling up his sleeves to work.
And... if the tragedy is to have no influence on the future,
happiness is to have at least a little.
For Joost and his citizens, Orania is a dream.
A dream born from the fear of disappearing, from the will to stay the same while everything around is changing.
It is the dream of those who want to protect a language, a faith, a past and write their own future without interference though.
But it is a fragile dream, because it stands on a border drawn to keep the other out.
The other who is different, distant, often difficult to understand.
And yet we are in South Africa.
A country where diversity is everywhere.
Where cultures mix and right there they find strength.
Maybe I did not understand everything and maybe some things escape me because I do not have their history.
I do not speak their language, but if in order to exist you have to exclude
and if to defend a culture every door to the world is shut,
well, then I do not know if this is a community or a fortress.
Because after all our travels around the world, I have understood that identity, if it is alive,
is not afraid of encounters.
It evolves, it transforms and it grows.
And maybe the only way not to get lost is to change.
But without ever stopping recognizing yourself.
